visual_checks: CRITICAL — VISUAL-TO-STRUCTURE COMPARISON: Look at the document and identify every piece of text that visually appears to be a heading (larger font, bold, different color, separated from body text). Then verify each one is tagged or styled as a proper heading, not just visually formatted.
Scan for text that is bold and/or larger than surrounding body text — does it use a built-in heading style (Heading 1, Heading 2, etc.) or is it just manually formatted?
Check the heading hierarchy: does the document start with H1, then use H2 for subsections, H3 for sub-subsections, and so on without skipping levels?

In PDFs, check the tag tree: are headings tagged as

,

,

, etc., or are they tagged as

with bold formatting?
In DOCX, check whether heading styles from the Styles gallery are used, not manual formatting (bold + larger font size on Normal style).
In PPTX, check that slide titles use the built-in Title placeholder, not a generic text box with large bold text.
Verify there are no empty headings (heading tags or styles applied to blank lines or spaces).
Look for the document title — it should be the highest-level heading (H1) or set as the document title in properties.
pass_conditions: All text that visually functions as a heading uses the appropriate built-in heading style or tag (H1-H6)
Heading levels follow a logical hierarchy without skipping levels (e.g., H1 followed by H2, not H1 followed by H3)
The document has at least one top-level heading (H1) that identifies the document or main topic
In PDFs, headings are tagged with the correct <H1>-<H6> tags in the tag tree
In DOCX files, Heading 1 through Heading 6 styles from the Styles gallery are used consistently
In PPTX files, slide titles use the built-in Title placeholder
No empty heading tags or styles are present
fail_conditions: Text that visually looks like a heading (bold, large, separated) is not tagged or styled as a heading — it uses body/normal/paragraph formatting with manual bold and font size changes
Heading levels are skipped (e.g., H1 jumps directly to H3 with no H2 in between)
The document has no headings at all despite having multiple sections or topics
All headings are the same level (e.g., everything is H2) despite a clear visual hierarchy of sections and subsections

In PDFs, text that looks like headings is tagged as

instead of

-
In PPTX, slide titles are in generic text boxes rather than the Title placeholder
Heading styles are applied to non-heading content (e.g., body paragraphs styled as Heading 2 to make them bold)
Empty headings exist (heading style applied to blank lines)
na_conditions: Document is a single short paragraph with no sections, subsections, or content that would logically require headings
Document is a simple form or single-page certificate with no hierarchical structure
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Document could not be opened or its heading structure could not be inspected
Document format does not support heading styles or tags
common_mistakes: Making text bold and large to look like a heading without applying a heading style — this is the most common document accessibility failure
Using Heading 1 for the title, then jumping to Heading 3 for subsections because the author preferred the visual style of Heading 3
Applying heading styles for visual effect on text that is not actually a heading (e.g., styling a quote as Heading 3 because it looks good)
Using all caps and bold on Normal-styled text instead of applying a heading style
In PPTX, deleting the Title placeholder and replacing it with a text box — the slide then has no programmatic title
Starting every page or section with H1 instead of using H1 once for the document title and H2 for top-level sections

examples.pass: A Word document uses Heading 1 for the document title, Heading 2 for each major section, and Heading 3 for subsections. The navigation pane shows the full heading outline matching the visual structure.
examples.fail: A Word document has text that looks like headings — bold, 16pt, with space above — but every paragraph uses the Normal style. The navigation pane is empty because no heading styles are applied. A screen reader user cannot jump between sections.
examples.partial:
A PDF has some headings properly tagged as

and

, but three section headings on pages 5-7 are tagged as

with bold text, breaking the navigable heading structure.
